source.title: Comparison of polysomnography in people with Alzheimer's disease and insomnia versus non-demented elderly people with insomnia

confidence_rationale: Large sample (n=837 elderly insomnia controls, n=239 AD+insomnia), objective PSG measurement with propensity score matching. Directly documents that AD is associated with reduced SWS%, fewer sleep spindles per minute N2, and lower EEG power in slow oscillation bands -- exactly the pipeline damage signature predicted by MECH-168. The non-significant trend for lower REM% in AD is partially supportive of MECH-173 but does not reach significance. Transfer risk: this is cross-sectional/diagnostic, so causal direction (AD causes SWS loss vs SWS loss causes AD) cannot be determined from this design alone.
failure_signatures: REM sleep reduction in AD did not reach statistical significance in this sample, weakening the MECH-173 Phase-4-first-failure prediction for this dataset (though the trend is in the predicted direction).
Cross-sectional design cannot determine whether SWS/spindle reductions preceded or followed AD neurodegeneration -- the causal directionality required by MECH-168 and MECH-174 is not testable here.

Both groups had insomnia at baseline -- the design effectively subtracts out insomnia, which is the intended strength, but means these findings reflect what AD adds to an insomnia baseline rather than AD vs healthy sleep.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Compared to elderly people with insomnia only, adults with AD and insomnia have: lower percentage of time in slow wave sleep (SWS), higher percentage in N1, fewer spindles per minute in N2 sleep, and lower absolute EEG power (particularly low-frequency bands). Trend for lower REM% in AD does not reach significance.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-168 predicts consolidation pipeline stages fail in reverse order: Phase 4 (precision recalibration, REM) first, then Phases 2-3 (schema replay, spindles), then Phase 1 (SWS homeostasis). This paper documents spindle reduction and SWS reduction in mild-moderate AD -- consistent with Phases 1-3 disruption. The non-significant REM trend is weaker evidence for Phase 4. The spindle reduction specifically maps to MECH-122 disruption (spindle coordination for schema packaging). MECH-174 predicts that SWS homeostasis and SWR replay are the phases disrupted by GABAergic agents; this paper documents these are also the phases disrupted in established AD.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper establishes the PSG signature of mild-moderate AD (not early MCI or prodromal stages) -- MECH-168 predicts Phase 4 failure precedes Phase 1/2/3 failure, so cross-sectional data from established AD cannot test the temporal ordering. The failure pattern here may represent late-stage pipeline damage, not early staging.
mapping.source_context: PSG comparison study using baseline data from Merck clinical trials; AD participants had mild-to-moderate severity; adults 55-80 years; propensity score matching for age and total sleep time.

confidence_components.notes: Transfer risk elevated due to cross-sectional design and established (not early/prodromal) AD. Does not test INV-047 staging prediction directly.
